content:
How Ribble Valley Pays: Payment Types and Methods

Key Findings

  • Ribble Valley Borough Council's total spending analysed for this period amounts to £38.6M.
  • Over five financial years, from 2021/22 to 2025/26, the council processed 13,723 transactions.
  • All of the council's analysed spending, a total of £38.6M, is categorised under the 'Unknown' payment type.
  • This 'Unknown' category accounts for 100.0% of all recorded payment types in the published data.

Analysing Ribble Valley's Financial Disclosures

AI DOGE has analysed Ribble Valley Borough Council's published spending data, covering a five-year period from the financial year 2021/22 through to 2025/26. This extensive review encompassed a total spend of £38.6M, spread across 13,723 individual transactions. The purpose of this analysis is to provide insight into how public funds are disbursed and to enhance transparency regarding local government expenditure. Understanding payment types is a crucial aspect of this transparency, as it can reveal operational efficiencies, preferred methods of payment, and areas for potential scrutiny.

Typically, local councils categorise their payments into various types, such as BACS transfers, credit card payments, direct debits, or cheques. These classifications offer valuable information about the council's financial operations and its relationships with suppliers. However, for Ribble Valley Borough Council, the data presents a unique situation. Our analysis shows that every single transaction within the £38.6M total spend is currently categorised as an 'Unknown' payment type. This means no specific method of payment is detailed in the publicly available records for any of the 13,723 transactions.

The Dominance of 'Unknown' Payment Types

The most striking finding from our review is the complete absence of specific payment type information. A substantial £38.6M, representing precisely 100.0% of all analysed spending by Ribble Valley Borough Council, falls under the 'Unknown' category. This categorisation applies to every transaction recorded in the council's spending data for the period 2021/22 to 2025/26. This lack of detail means that the public, and indeed analysts, cannot discern whether the council primarily uses electronic transfers, traditional cheques, procurement cards, or other payment methods for its expenditures.

The implications of having all payments classified as 'Unknown' are significant for financial transparency. Without specific payment type data, it becomes challenging to assess the efficiency of the council's payment processes. For example, electronic payments like BACS transfers are often more cost-effective and secure than paper cheques. The absence of this information prevents any analysis of such operational aspects. Furthermore, detailed payment types can sometimes provide additional layers of insight into financial controls and potential areas of risk, which are currently obscured by the 'Unknown' classification.

Implications for Transparency and Public Scrutiny

The Local Government Transparency Code encourages councils to publish spending data in a way that is accessible and understandable to the public. This includes providing sufficient detail to allow for meaningful scrutiny of how public money is spent. While Ribble Valley Borough Council has published its transaction data, the universal 'Unknown' categorisation for payment types limits the depth of analysis possible under the Code's spirit. The ability to identify specific payment methods would empower residents to ask more targeted questions about financial management and accountability.

This situation highlights a gap in the publicly available information that could otherwise contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of the council's financial operations. Without knowing the actual payment methods for £38.6M of public spending, it is difficult for citizens to fully scrutinise the council's financial practices. Enhanced detail in this area would allow for a clearer picture of how funds are moved from the council to its suppliers, promoting greater trust and accountability in local government spending.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Ribble Valley Borough Council about the categorisation of payment types in their spending data.
  • Attend council committee meetings (they are open to the public) to raise questions about financial transparency and payment method reporting.
  • Check the council's published contracts register for additional details on how specific services are procured and paid for.
  • Write to your local councillor about the importance of detailed payment type information in public spending records.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and compare Ribble Valley Borough Council's disclosures with other Lancashire councils.
This article is based on Ribble Valley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
